title: When Should You Order Advanced Lipoprotein Testing?

items.title: Why standard cholesterol panels miss high-risk patients
items.text: ACC/AHA risk calculators based on total and HDL cholesterol can fail to identify individuals at high ASCVD risk. Proatherogenic lipoprotein subclasses—small dense LDL and triglyceride-rich remnants—and low large HDL particles may drive risk disproportionate to measured cholesterol content.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "the algorithm can fail to identify individuals at high risk for ASCVD. Thus, candidates for lipid-lowering on the basis of plasma levels of lipoprotein particle subclasses may not be identified."

items.title: Components of advanced lipoprotein testing
items.text: Advanced testing includes nuclear magnetic resonance or ion mobility particle quantification, total LDL particle and/or apolipoprotein B concentrations, and lipoprotein(a) measured by immunoassay. Together these refine ASCVD risk beyond standard lipid panels.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Collectively, these measurements, as well as plasma levels of total LDL particles and/or apolipoprotein B, comprise advanced lipoprotein testing."

items.title: How to select patients for advanced lipoprotein testing
items.text: Consider advanced testing for borderline dyslipidemia or intermediate estimated risk, metabolic syndrome features, and significant family history of ASCVD. It also helps assess response to lipid-lowering therapy.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Consideration should be given to obtaining such testing for ASCVD risk assessment in individuals with borderline dyslipidemia or estimated risk using current algorithms, features of metabolic syndrome, and/or significant family history of ASCVD."

items.title: Why LDL cholesterol misestimates LDL particle burden
items.text: LDL particles play a central causal role in ASCVD, but total and LDL cholesterol can over- or underestimate LDL particle concentrations because LDL exists across a spectrum of size and cholesterol content.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "measurement of total and LDL cholesterol can lead to overestimation or underestimation of LDL particle concentrations. This is because there is a spectrum of LDL"

items.title: Nuclear magnetic resonance versus ion mobility for LDL subclasses
items.text: NMR deconvolves a single plasma lipid signal into size components; ion mobility directly measures particle subclass concentrations in defined size intervals. Reference ranges differ, and ion mobility links both medium and small LDL to ASCVD risk.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The former is based on mathematical deconvolution of a single lipid-generated signal from plasma into multiple components of differing size, while the latter yields direct measurement of concentrations of lipoprotein particle subclasses within defined particle size intervals."

items.title: Why small LDL varies independently of LDL cholesterol
items.text: Small and medium LDL particle levels can vary greatly at any given LDL cholesterol or triglyceride concentration. Correlations are stronger with non-HDL cholesterol and apoB, but substantial divergence remains—advanced testing is preferred for subclass profiling.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Levels of these particles, and in particular small LDL, can vary greatly at any given plasma concentration of LDL cholesterol or triglyceride"

items.title: Why lipoprotein(a) requires separate immunoassay measurement
items.text: Lp(a) is not readily discriminated by NMR or ion mobility. It consists of LDL covalently linked to apolipoprotein(a), conferring prothrombotic and proatherogenic properties beyond LDL cholesterol content, including stroke, CAD, and calcific aortic stenosis risk.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Another key component of advanced lipoprotein testing is Lp(a). This particle is not readily discriminated by nuclear magnetic resonance or ion mobility techniques and thus is most commonly measured by immunoassay."

items.title: How to approach elevated Lp(a) in families
items.text: Lp(a) is strongly genetically determined via LPA polymorphisms and kringle-repeat variation. More than one-third of the population exceeds 75 nmol/L; cascade screening of first-degree relatives directs aggressive ASCVD prevention in affected relatives.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Due to the strong genetic basis of elevated Lp(a), cascade screening of first-degree family members for this condition has value for directing more aggressive efforts at ASCVD reduction in the 50% of such relatives who may be affected."

items.title: Pitfall: lack of RCTs targeting particle subspecies
items.text: Therapeutic decisions are limited by absence of RCTs proving particle-subspecies reduction superior to LDL-C lowering for event reduction. Nevertheless, total LDL particle and apoB concentrations predict risk in multiple analyses.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "the application of evidence-based criteria for therapeutic decision-making has been limited by the lack of randomized control trials aimed at specifically testing whether reductions in lipoprotein particle subspecies are superior to change in LDL cholesterol"

items.title: Four ACC/AHA groups warranting cholesterol-lowering therapy
items.text: Candidates include clinical ASCVD; LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL; diabetes age 40–75; and adults 40–75 without ASCVD/diabetes with ≥7.5% 10-year ASCVD risk (consider 5–7.5% intermediate risk).
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "4 groups of candidates for cholesterol-lowering therapy: (1) clinical ASCVD; (2) LDL cholesterol ≥190 mg/dL (≥4.92 mmol/L); (3) diabetes mellitus (patients aged 40 to 75 years); and (4) individuals aged 40 to 75 years without ASCVD or diabetes who have a 10-year risk of ASCVD of at least 7.5%"

items.title: How dietary carbohydrate restriction lowers small LDL
items.text: For patients with elevated small LDL and pattern B, limiting carbohydrate rather than saturated fat is most appropriate to reduce small dense LDL particles—a prominent feature of advanced panel abnormalities.
items.reference: Case 1: "Limitation of carbohydrate rather than saturated fat (Answer A) is most appropriate for reducing elevated levels of small LDL particles, which is a prominent feature of this patient's lipoprotein profile."

items.title: Why clinicians underuse advanced lipoprotein testing
items.text: Without particle measurements, clinicians may miss patients needing more aggressive lipid management. Challenges include differing assay platforms and lack of standardized risk thresholds, but experience with methodology enables effective use in selected patients.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Without obtaining measurements of lipoprotein particles that comprise advanced lipid testing, clinicians may not recognize the advisability of more aggressive lipid management for a significant subset of their patients."

items.question: A 43-year-old woman has total cholesterol 206 mg/dL, LDL-C 136 mg/dL, HDL-C 49 mg/dL, and 10-year ASCVD risk 5.8%. Advanced testing shows high total/small/medium LDL particles, pattern B, and Lp(a) 173 nmol/L. What initial pharmacotherapy is recommended?
options: Diet only with further saturated fat restriction
items.explanation: Statins remain first-line for increased ASCVD risk. Carbohydrate restriction—not saturated fat alone—targets small LDL; niacin and PCSK9 inhibitors are adjunctive after maximal statin therapy or intolerance.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: D) A statin"

items.question: After rosuvastatin 10 mg daily for 3 months, small LDL fell 45% and medium LDL 37%, but levels remain above optimal with persistent elevated Lp(a) and pattern B. What is the best interpretation?
options: Statin therapy failed; stop the statin
Partial response warrants ongoing lipid management beyond initial statin
No further therapy is needed because LDL-C may be at goal
items.explanation: Statin produced meaningful subclass reductions but residual high-risk particles and Lp(a) persist—further management is warranted.
items.reference: Case 1: "the resulting levels were above optimal, and elevated Lp(a) and LDL subclass pattern B persisted. Therefore, further lipid management was felt to be warranted and is underway."

items.question: At what Lp(a) concentration does the chapter cite increased ASCVD risk in more than one-third of the population?
items.explanation: Plasma Lp(a) >75 nmol/L exceeds risk-associated levels in more than one-third of the population, with markedly higher relative risk at the 95th percentile.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "plasma Lp(a) concentrations in more than one-third of the population are higher than those associated with increased ASCVD risk (>75 nmol/L)"

items.question: When might nicotinic acid still be considered after statin therapy in a patient with elevated Lp(a)?
options: Only if LDL-C is at goal on maximal statin ± ezetimibe but Lp(a) and subclasses remain high
Only in patients with LDL-C <70 mg/dL and no family history
items.explanation: Niacin may reduce Lp(a) and small LDL but failed to add clinical benefit in large trials atop maximal statin therapy; it may still be trialed if substantial residual abnormalities persist.
items.reference: Case 1: "if this patient does not achieve substantial further improvement in LDL levels, nicotinic acid may be subsequently considered for a trial of adjunctive therapy."

items.question: When are PCSK9 inhibitors appropriately indicated per this chapter?
options: For any patient with elevated Lp(a) before statin trial
Addition to maximal statin therapy when LDL lowering is insufficient or after documented statin intolerance
As monotherapy for intermediate 10-year risk only
items.explanation: PCSK9 inhibitors are indicated for addition to maximal statin therapy when LDL lowering is insufficient or with intolerance to multiple statins.
items.reference: Case 1: "PCSK9 inhibitors (Answer C) are currently indicated only for addition to maximal statin therapy if LDL lowering is not sufficient or if there is intolerance to multiple statins."

items.question: Which advanced measures does the chapter cite as supporting ASCVD risk prediction?
options: Total LDL particle concentrations and apolipoprotein B
Remnant cholesterol validated for routine risk assignment
items.explanation: Analyses support total LDL particle concentrations and apoB for risk prediction; remnant particle levels are not yet established for routine risk assessment.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "a number of analyses have provided support for the power of total LDL particle concentrations, as well as apolipoprotein B in this regard."

items.question: A 52-year-old man has 10-year ASCVD risk 6.2% with metabolic syndrome and strong family history of premature MI. Standard lipids are borderline. What is the best next laboratory step?
options: No further testing; risk is below 7.5%
Advanced lipoprotein testing including Lp(a) and LDL subclasses
Coronary calcium score only—particle testing never adds value
items.explanation: Intermediate risk (5–7.5%) with metabolic syndrome and family history is an appropriate setting for advanced lipoprotein testing.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Consideration should be given to obtaining such testing for ASCVD risk assessment in individuals with borderline dyslipidemia or estimated risk using current algorithms, features of metabolic syndrome, and/or significant family history of ASCVD."

items.question: Which pathologic property of Lp(a) is highlighted beyond its LDL component?
options: Competitive inhibition of plasminogen-induced clot lysis
items.explanation: Apo(a) confers prothrombotic effects via plasminogen competition and increased oxidizability via disulfide bonds.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "These include a prothrombotic effect due to its competitive inhibition of plasminogen-induced clot lysis"

items.question: Ion mobility analysis links ASCVD risk to which LDL fractions?
options: IDL remnants only—LDL size is irrelevant
items.explanation: Ion mobility data associate risk with medium-sized as well as small LDL particles.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "In the case of ion mobility, ASCVD risk has been found to be associated with concentrations of medium-sized, as well as small, LDL particles."

items.question: A patient has LDL subclass pattern B with small LDL 394 nmol/L (high) and LDL peak size 216.8 Å. Which dietary emphasis is most aligned with the case discussion?
options: Further saturated fat restriction as sole intervention
Carbohydrate limitation to reduce small dense LDL
items.explanation: Carbohydrate restriction—not saturated fat limitation alone—is preferred for elevated small LDL/pattern B.
items.reference: Case 1: "Limitation of carbohydrate rather than saturated fat (Answer A) is most appropriate for reducing elevated levels of small LDL particles"

items.question: A patient has Lp(a) 173 nmol/L and premature CAD. What family-based strategy does the chapter endorse?
options: No family testing—Lp(a) is entirely environmental
Screen siblings only if LDL-C exceeds 190 mg/dL
items.explanation: Cascade screening of first-degree relatives helps identify the ~50% who may share elevated Lp(a) and need intensified prevention.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "cascade screening of first-degree family members for this condition has value for directing more aggressive efforts at ASCVD reduction in the 50% of such relatives who may be affected."

items.question: In the case table, large HDL particles were 4086 nmol/L. How does the chapter frame large HDL particles?
options: Always adequately captured by HDL cholesterol
items.explanation: Large HDL particles are associated with reduced ASCVD risk; advanced panels quantify them beyond standard HDL-C.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "large HDL particles, which are associated with reduced ASCVD risk."

items.question: Despite LDL-lowering therapy efficacy, why does considerable residual ASCVD risk persist?
options: All risk is explained by LDL cholesterol
Proatherogenic particles such as small dense LDL and Lp(a) impact risk disproportionate to their cholesterol content
HDL cholesterol elevation always eliminates residual risk
items.explanation: Residual risk may not be reflected by LDL-C due to atherogenic particles whose risk exceeds their cholesterol content.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "This may not be reflected by the plasma LDL-cholesterol concentration due to increased levels of lipoprotein particles, in particular small, dense LDL and Lp(a), that impact ASCVD to an extent disproportionate to their cholesterol content."

items.statement: ACC/AHA risk calculators always identify every patient who would benefit from lipid-lowering therapy.
items.explanation: The algorithm can fail to identify high-risk individuals whose risk is driven by lipoprotein subclasses.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "the algorithm can fail to identify individuals at high risk for ASCVD."

items.statement: Lp(a) is most commonly measured by immunoassay rather than NMR or ion mobility.
items.explanation: Lp(a) is not readily discriminated by NMR or ion mobility techniques.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "is most commonly measured by immunoassay."

items.statement: Remnant lipoprotein particle levels measured by advanced assays are established for routine ASCVD risk assignment.
items.explanation: Remnant levels are measured but not yet established for application in ASCVD risk assessment.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "their levels are not yet established for application in ASCVD risk assessment."

items.statement: Statins remain the first-line pharmacologic intervention for patients at increased ASCVD risk.
items.explanation: Based on well-established efficacy, statins are first-line for increased-risk patients.
items.reference: Case 1: "statins remain the first-line pharmacologic intervention for all patients considered to be at increased risk of ASCVD."

items.statement: Elevated plasma Lp(a) is associated with risk for calcific aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: Lp(a) is linked to stroke, CAD, and calcific aortic stenosis.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "plasma Lp(a) is also associated with risk for calcific aortic stenosis."

items.statement: Nicotinic acid added to maximal statin ± ezetimibe consistently improved clinical outcomes in recent large trials.
items.explanation: Niacin failed to produce clinical benefit in recent large trials when added to maximal LDL lowering.
items.reference: Case 1: "has failed to produce clinical benefit in recent large trials when added to a regimen yielding maximal LDL reduction by statins ± ezetimibe."

items.statement: A 10-year ASCVD risk of 5% to 7.5% may warrant consideration for cholesterol-lowering therapy per ACC/AHA guidance cited.
items.explanation: Consideration may be given to intermediate 10-year risk of 5% to 7.5%.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Consideration may also be given to intermediate 10-year risk of 5% to 7.5%."

items.statement: Changes in small LDL particles are strongly correlated with changes in triglyceride-rich lipoproteins and HDL.
items.explanation: Small LDL changes track with triglyceride-rich lipoprotein and HDL changes, complicating isolated subclass attribution.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "changes in small LDL particles in particular are strongly correlated with changes in triglyceride-rich lipoproteins and HDL."

items.assertion: Assertion: Advanced lipoprotein testing can identify ASCVD risk not revealed by standard lipid measurements.
items.reason: Reason: Small dense LDL and Lp(a) confer risk disproportionate to their cholesterol content.
items.explanation: Both are true and causally linked to residual risk beyond LDL-C.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "can identify individuals at heightened ASCVD risk that is not revealed by standard lipid measurements"

items.assertion: Assertion: A statin is the appropriate initial pharmacologic therapy for the case patient with intermediate risk and abnormal advanced panel.
items.reason: Reason: PCSK9 inhibitors are indicated as first-line monotherapy for intermediate-risk patients.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; PCSK9 inhibitors require maximal statin therapy or intolerance—not first-line monotherapy here.
items.reference: Case 1: "PCSK9 inhibitors (Answer C) are currently indicated only for addition to maximal statin therapy"

items.assertion: Assertion: Cascade family screening is valuable when Lp(a) is elevated.
items.reason: Reason: Lp(a) levels are under strong genetic control with wide population variability.
items.explanation: Both are true; genetic basis supports family screening.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Lp(a) levels are under much stronger genetic control than other plasma lipoproteins"

items.assertion: Assertion: Carbohydrate restriction is preferred over saturated fat restriction for elevated small LDL.
items.reason: Reason: Saturated fat is the primary driver of small dense LDL in all patients.
items.explanation: Assertion matches the case; saturated fat as sole driver is false for this pattern.
items.reference: Case 1: "Limitation of carbohydrate rather than saturated fat (Answer A) is most appropriate for reducing elevated levels of small LDL particles"

items.assertion: Assertion: Apolipoprotein B concentrations support ASCVD risk assessment.
items.reason: Reason: ApoB measures the number of atherogenic particles.
items.explanation: Both are true; apoB reflects particle number cited in the chapter analyses.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "provided support for the power of total LDL particle concentrations, as well as apolipoprotein B"

items.assertion: Assertion: NMR and ion mobility use identical analytic principles.
items.reason: Reason: NMR deconvolves a lipid signal while ion mobility directly measures particle concentrations in size intervals.
items.explanation: Assertion is false; the reason correctly describes different methodologies.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The former is based on mathematical deconvolution of a single lipid-generated signal from plasma into multiple components of differing size, while the latter yields direct measurement"

items.assertion: Assertion: Mendelian randomization supports causality for Lp(a) and LDL cholesterol in ASCVD.
items.reason: Reason: Mendelian randomization supports causality for HDL cholesterol levels in ASCVD.
items.explanation: Lp(a) and LDL-C are supported; HDL-C causality is not supported in this discussion.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Mendelian randomization, which has been used to infer ASCVD causality for LDL-cholesterol and triglyceride levels, as well as Lp(a) (but not HDL-cholesterol) levels"

items.assertion: Assertion: Without advanced particle testing clinicians may undertreat a subset of at-risk patients.
items.reason: Reason: All methodological platforms for advanced lipoprotein testing are fully standardized with universal risk cutoffs.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; lack of standardization is a barrier—the reason is false.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "there are some challenges for implementing such testing in practice, including differing methodological platforms and the lack of standardized criteria for assigning ASCVD risk."
